subject: [PATCH 00/13] mm: PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs

reviews.author: Kairui Song
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Kairui Song thanked Usama Arif for his work, indicating a positive sentiment.

Reviewer Kairui Song noted that swap_map metadata can be reduced further by storing a swp_tb entry directly in ci->table, and suggested doing this later as part of cluster compaction.

Reviewer Kairui Song noted a potential conflict between this patch series and the swap folio allocation unification, specifically mentioning that the swap_cache_alloc_folio helper can be used to directly allocate 2M folios using PMD_ORDER. They suggested checking the linked patch for details and proposed updating their own series to disable forced order 0 fallback and allow callers to pass in a specific THP order.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Usama,

Thanks for the work!

---

swap_map is gone, metadata is still per slot but with PMD sized
swapout, I think soon we can store a swp_tb entry directly in
ci->table (make it a union maybe) so the metadata is significantly
reduced from there too. Better do that later with cluster compaction.

---

There is a slight conflict with the swap folio allocation unification,
which should be easy to solve. Just a little head up, check the
swap_cache_alloc_folio helper here:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260421-swap-table-p4-v3-4-2f23759a76bc@tencent.com/

We will be able to directly allocate 2M folios using
swap_cache_alloc_folio(orders = BIT(PMD_ORDER)) in the patch link
above. Might even help to avoid issues with splitting or raced swapin?

The conflict can be solved from either side, I'll update that series to
disable the forced order 0 fallback and let caller pass in (orders =
<mTHP order> | BIT(0)) instead.

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary: The reviewer expressed their intention to review the patch after they finish working on another project and have some time in May.
reviews.raw_body:
My email is a disaster right now, various other stuff + lately working hard on
the thing-I'm-going-to-talk-about-at-LSF and the-slides-for-that has left me
with only backlog but... :) will want to have a look post-LSF also. But May
likely to be slow for me alos.

Cheers, Lorenzo

reviews.author: Zi Yan
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Zi Yan did not raise any specific technical issues or concerns about the patch, but instead mentioned they would likely review it later due to being mostly AFK in May.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a PMD swap entry is split into PTEs due to zswap or non-contiguous swap allocation, and requested that the fallback path be updated to correctly handle this scenario.
reviews.raw_body:
Thank you for the confirmation. I will be mostly AFK in May and will
probably check the patches later.

---

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

2026-04-29.patch_summary: This series of patches introduces PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs, allowing huge mappings to be preserved across the swap round-trip and reducing memory usage when swapping. The existing PTE-split path is still used as a fallback in cases where PMD-order resources are unavailable. This change has benefits including faster fault handling on swap-in, reduced memory allocation during swap, and improved performance for walkers such as zap and mprotect.

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary:
Author agrees to restructure the swapoff path in v2 to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), addressing a concern about locking.

Author agrees to remove order 0 fallback in two specific failure cases, indicating a fix is planned for future revisions.
reviews.raw_body:
Oh yeah, I like your swapin_alloc_pmd_folio a lot more than
swapin_alloc_pmd_folio.

---

Yes, that would be great. We dont want order 0 fallback in the 2 cases
where we fail in this series.

Thanks!

2026-04-30.patch_summary: This series of patches introduces PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs, allowing huge mappings to be preserved across the swap round-trip and reducing memory usage when swapping. The existing PTE-split path is still used as a fallback in cases where PMD-order resources are unavailable. This change has benefits including faster fault handling on swap-in, reduced memory allocation during swap, and improved performance for walkers such as zap and mprotect.
